name: Alocasia longiloba 'Watsoniana Pink Doff Variegated'
origin: China to western and central Malesia; variegated collector form in cultivation
aboutText: Alocasia longiloba 'Watsoniana Pink Doff Variegated' is an exceptionally beautiful, corm-forming understory aroid native to China and western Malesia. Known for its massive, stiff shield leaves with contrasting white primary veins, it features a velvety green surface with a dark metallic sheen. The highly sought-after 'Pink Doff' cultivar exhibits stunning splashes of soft pink variegation, requiring warm, highly humid forest-floor conditions with consistent moisture and filtered light.

morphology.leafShape: Elongated sagittate with pronounced posterior lobes
morphology.variegation: Soft pink marbling and sectoral patches

propagation.notes: Wait for the mother plant to produce corms or pups before dividing. Larger, well-established plants produce offsets most readily. Alocasias do not propagate reliably from stem cuttings.

fieldNotes.title: On the Curious Case of Pink and Velour
fieldNotes.author: Aroid Aaron
fieldNotes.content: Right, another brew in hand, admiring something utterly magnificent – and frankly, quite costly – the Alocasia longiloba 'Watsoniana Pink Doff Variegated'. A splendid corm-forming chap from China to Malesia's understory. Its massive, stiff, elongated sagittate leaves, with their rather pronounced posterior lobes, boast a dark, velvety green with a metallic sheen and striking silvery-white veins. But the real madness? The utterly delightful soft pink marbling and sectoral patches. Requires endless misting in my drafty glasshouse to mimic its consistently moist, filtered light forest-floor. Still, one does make sacrifices for such botanical artistry, wouldn't you agree?
